content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

August 6, 1976

Robert N. Tait
District Attorney
Courthouse Annex
San Luis Obispo, California 93401

Dear Mr. Tait:

     Thank you for your letter of July 26, 1976, in which
you asked the following questions:

     "Does Government Code Section 87100, as interpreted
by 2 California Administrative Code Sections 18700(e)
and (f), prohibit a public official after such official
has disqualified himself from participating in a
decision before his agency, from appearing before
his or her agency: (1) to speak as a private citizen
from the audience, or (2) to represent himself or
herself on matters related solely to his or her
personal interest(?)"

     The answer to both of the above questions is "no".
Like yourself, the Commission recognized the free speech
implications of the prohibitions of the Political Reform
Act. The Commission's concern for the First Amendment led
it to adopt 2 Cal. Adm. Code §18700(f).

     If you could be more specific with respect to the
perceived ambiguity in the Section, please contact us.

                                        Sincerely,


                                        Delbert L. Spurlock, Jr.,
                                        Chief Conflicts of Interest
                                        Division
                                        916/322-6444

DLS;jhm




TECHNICAL ASSISTANCE ADMINISTRATION (916) 322-5660 • EXECUTIVE LEGAL 322-5901 • ENFORCEMENT CONFLICT OF INTEREST 322-6441

ROBERT N. TAIT
District Attorney
Telephone 543-3464

DISTRICT ATTORNEY
Courthouse Annex
SAN LUIS OBISPO, CALIFORNIA 93401

July 26, 1976


Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. Box 807
1100 "K" St.
Sacramento, CA  95804

Re:  Opinion Request

Gentlemen:

Pursuant to California Administrative Code Sections 18320
and 18329, written advice is hereby requested with respect
to the following question:

Does Government Code Section 87100, as interpreted by 2
California Administrative Code Sections 18700(e) and (f),
prohibit a public official after such official has disqual-
ified himself from participating in a decision before his
agency, from appearing before his or her agency: (1) to
speak as a private citizen from the audience, or (2) to
represent himself or herself on matters related solely to
his or her personal interest.

Our question is motivated by what we perceive to be some
ambiguity in Section 18700 and our most serious respect for
the First Amendment. A prompt response to this inquiry is
requested inasmuch as we are in the process of drafting
Conflict of Interest Codes for various local government
agencies.

Very truly yours,

ROBERT N. TAIT
District Attorney

RNT:ks

sections.conclusion: The answer to your questions is 'no'.
sections.question_synthetic: Whether Government Code Section 87100 prohibits a disqualified public official from appearing before their agency either to speak as a private citizen or to represent themselves on personal matters.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC concludes that Government Code Section 87100 does not prohibit a disqualified public official from appearing before their agency as a private citizen or to represent themselves on personal matters, recognizing First Amendment implications.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: modern; LLM: Document contains both incoming request (July 26, 1976 letter from DA Tait) and FPPC response (undated but appears to be from June 1976 based on context). Response quality is somewhat degraded due to OCR/document quality issues, but core Q&C sections are identifiable. Extracted response portions which represent FPPC's official advice.

embedding.summary: FPPC advice letter responding to San Luis Obispo District Attorney's request for opinion on whether disqualified public officials are prohibited from appearing before their agency as private citizens or to represent personal interests. The FPPC answers in the negative, citing First Amendment concerns.
